ai_wrong: Students often think the torque is the same, which is only true for shafts in series.
trick: Parallel = Same twist; Series = Same torque.

question: A tensile test is performed on a round bar. After fracture, it has been found that the diameter remains approximately the same at fracture. The material under test was
explanation: The material under test was cast iron because cast iron exhibits a brittle fracture with little or no necking, resulting in a fracture surface with a diameter similar to that of the original bar.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Cast iron is a brittle material that fails suddenly without significant plastic deformation, so the diameter remains nearly constant.
ai_wrong: Students often pick mild steel (Option A), which shows significant necking (reduction in diameter) before fracture.
trick: Brittle materials show no necking.

question: The compressive strength of brittle materials is
explanation: The compressive strength of brittle materials is greater than their tensile strength. This is because brittle materials are more resistant to compression than to tension, which causes them to fail more easily in tension.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Brittle materials are much stronger in compression than in tension because compressive forces tend to close micro-cracks rather than propagate them.
ai_wrong: Students often assume strength is the same in both directions, which is only true for some ductile materials.
trick: Brittle = Stronger in compression.

question: Two shafts 'A' and 'B' transmit the same power. The speed of shaft 'A' is 250 r.p.m. and that of shaft 'B' is 300 r.p.m. The statement that shaft 'B' has the greater diameter is
explanation: The statement is false because if two shafts transmit the same power, the one with the higher speed will have a smaller diameter. This is due to the relationship between power, speed, and torque, where power = torque * speed. For the same power, a higher speed results in a lower torque, which can be achieved with a smaller diameter shaft.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Power P = T * ω. Since P is constant, T = P/ω. Shaft B has higher speed (ω), so it requires less torque, meaning it can have a smaller diameter.
ai_wrong: Students often assume higher speed requires a larger diameter, ignoring the inverse relationship between torque and speed.
trick: Higher speed = Lower torque = Smaller diameter.

question: A welded joint as compared to a riveted joint has
explanation: A welded joint as compared to a riveted joint has less strength. This is because welded joints can be prone to defects such as porosity, lack of fusion, and cracking, which can reduce their strength. Additionally, the heat-affected zone in a welded joint can also lead to a reduction in strength.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Welded joints are generally more efficient and can achieve higher joint efficiency (often approaching 100%) compared to riveted joints.
ai_wrong: Students often think riveted joints are stronger due to their physical bulk, but welding provides a continuous connection.
trick: Welded = More efficient.

question: The shear force of a cantilever beam of length l carrying a uniformly distributed load of w per unit length is
explanation: The shear force of a cantilever beam of length l carrying a uniformly distributed load of w per unit length is zero at the free end. This is because the shear force is the sum of all the forces acting on the beam to one side of a given point, and at the free end, there are no forces acting on the beam.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For a cantilever beam with UDL, the shear force is maximum at the fixed end and zero at the free end.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse the shear force at the free end with the bending moment, which is also zero.
trick: Cantilever free end: SF = 0, BM = 0.

question: The bending moment at a point on a beam is the algebraic
explanation: The bending moment at a point on a beam is the algebraic sum of the moments of all the forces acting on the beam.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Bending moment is the algebraic sum of the moments of all forces to one side of the section, but it is defined as the difference between the clockwise and counter-clockwise moments.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with the definition of shear force, which is an algebraic sum.
trick: BM is the net moment.

question: In the Euler's formula, the value of C for a column with one end fixed and the other end hinged, is 1 / 2.
explanation: In the Euler's formula, the value of C for a column with one end fixed and the other end hinged, is 1 / 2.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The effective length for a fixed-hinged column is Le = L/√2 ≈ 0.707L. The value 1/2 is incorrect.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse the coefficients for different end conditions.
trick: Fixed-Hinged = 0.7L; Fixed-Fixed = 0.5L.

question: Every direct stress is always accompanied by a strain in its own direction and an opposite kind of strain in every direction, at right angles to it. Such a strain is known as
explanation: Every direct stress is always accompanied by a strain in its own direction and an opposite kind of strain in every direction, at right angles to it. Such a strain is known as lateral strain.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Lateral strain is the strain occurring in the direction perpendicular to the applied direct stress, caused by the Poisson effect.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with linear strain (Option A), which is in the direction of the load.
trick: Direct stress = Linear strain; Perpendicular = Lateral strain.

question: The bending stress in a beam is
explanation: The bending stress in a beam is directly proportional to the bending moment and inversely proportional to the section modulus.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The flexure formula σ = My/I shows that bending stress is directly proportional to the distance from the neutral axis (y) and the bending moment (M).
ai_wrong: Students often think it is inversely proportional to the moment of inertia, which is true, but 'directly proportional' is the standard relationship to M.
trick: Bending stress is linear with distance from the neutral axis.

question: The section modulus (Z) of a beam is given by
explanation: The section modulus (Z) is defined as the ratio of the moment of inertia (I) of the beam's cross-section about its neutral axis to the distance (y) of the extreme fiber from the neutral axis. Thus, Z = I / y.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Section modulus Z is defined as the ratio of the moment of inertia (I) to the distance of the extreme fiber from the neutral axis (y).
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with the formula for bending stress (M/I).
trick: Z = I/y.

question: According to Euler's column theory, the crippling load for a column of length (l) with one end fixed and the other end free is compared to a column with both ends hinged. The crippling load is:
explanation: Euler's crippling load is given by P = (π²EI) / (Le²). For a column with one end fixed and one free, the effective length (Le) is 2l, making the load P = (π²EI) / (4l²). For a column with both ends hinged, Le = l, making the load P = (π²EI) / (l²). Therefore, the load for the fixed-free column is less than that of the hinged-hinged column.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The crippling load P = π^2EI / Le^2. For fixed-free, Le = 2L (P ∝ 1/4L^2). For hinged-hinged, Le = L (P ∝ 1/L^2). Thus, fixed-free is less.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse the effective length factors, leading to the wrong comparison.
trick: Fixed-Free is the weakest column configuration.

question: A shear stress across a plane is always accompanied by a balancing shear stress across the plane perpendicular to it. This statement is:
explanation: According to the principle of complementary shear stress, a shear stress acting on a plane must be accompanied by an equal shear stress acting on a perpendicular plane to maintain static equilibrium (preventing rotation of the element).
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Complementary shear stress theorem states that shear stress on one plane is accompanied by an equal shear stress on the perpendicular plane.
ai_wrong: Students often think shear stress can exist in isolation, which violates equilibrium.
trick: Shear always comes in pairs.

question: Two closely coiled helical springs 'A' and 'B' are equal in all respects but the number of turns of spring 'A' is half that of spring 'B'. The ratio of deflections in spring 'A' to spring 'B' is
explanation: The deflection (δ) of a helical spring is given by δ = (8PD³n) / (Gd⁴). Since all parameters except the number of turns (n) are equal, deflection is directly proportional to n. If nA = 0.5 * nB, then δA / δB = nA / nB = 0.5, which is 1:2.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Deflection δ = (64 * W * R^3 * n) / (G * d^4). Since δ is proportional to n (number of turns), half the turns means half the deflection.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with stiffness (k), which is inversely proportional to n.
trick: Deflection is directly proportional to the number of turns.

question: A lap joint is always in
explanation: In a lap joint, two plates overlap and are joined by fasteners. The load is transferred through a single shear plane across the fasteners, resulting in single shear.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: In a lap joint, the plates overlap, and the load path creates a single shear plane through the rivet.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with a butt joint, which can be in double shear.
trick: Lap = Single shear.

question: The shear force and bending moment are zero at the free end of a cantilever beam if it carries a:
options: point load at the free end
point load at the middle of its length
uniformly distributed load over the whole length
explanation: For a cantilever beam, the shear force and bending moment at the free end are zero only if there is no load applied at that specific point. If a point load is applied at the free end, the shear force is equal to that load and the bending moment is zero. If the load is applied elsewhere, the free end remains unloaded.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For a cantilever beam, both shear force and bending moment are zero at the free end regardless of the loading type, as there are no forces beyond that point.
ai_wrong: Students often think the load type changes the boundary condition at the free end.
trick: Free end of cantilever always has zero SF and BM.

question: In a thick cylindrical shell subjected to an internal pressure (p), the radial stress across the thickness of the cylinder is:
options: maximum at the outer surface and minimum at the inner surface
maximum at the inner surface and minimum at the outer surface
maximum at the outer surface and zero at the inner surface
maximum at the inner surface and zero at the outer surface
explanation: According to Lame's equations for thick cylinders, the radial stress is equal to the internal pressure at the inner surface (r = ri) and is zero at the outer surface (r = ro) assuming no external pressure is applied.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: In a thick cylinder, radial stress is compressive and varies from -p at the inner surface to 0 at the outer surface.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse radial stress with hoop stress, which is maximum at the inner surface.
trick: Radial stress is zero at the outer surface.

question: The torsional rigidity of a shaft is defined as the product of the modulus of rigidity (G) and the polar moment of inertia (J). It is represented by the expression:
explanation: Torsional rigidity is defined as the torque required to produce a unit angle of twist per unit length. From the torsion equation T/J = G*theta/L, we get T/theta = G*J/L. For a unit length, torsional rigidity is G*J.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Torsional rigidity is the torque required to produce a unit angle of twist, defined as T/θ = GJ/L.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with the product GJ, which is the torsional stiffness.
trick: Rigidity = Torque per unit twist.

question: In a tensile test, when the material is stressed beyond the elastic limit, the tensile strain:
explanation: Once the material passes the elastic limit and enters the plastic deformation region (yielding and strain hardening), the material undergoes permanent deformation, causing the tensile strain to increase more rapidly relative to the applied stress.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Beyond the elastic limit, the material enters the plastic region where strain increases rapidly with very little increase in stress.
ai_wrong: Students often think strain decreases, but plastic deformation is characterized by permanent elongation.
trick: Beyond elastic limit = Rapid strain increase.

question: Within the elastic limit, shear stress is:
explanation: According to Hooke's Law for shear, within the elastic limit, the shear stress is directly proportional to the shear strain. The constant of proportionality is known as the modulus of rigidity (G).
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Hooke's law states that stress is directly proportional to strain within the elastic limit.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with the yield point or plastic limit.
trick: Hooke's Law = Proportionality.

question: A cantilever beam of length L carries a gradually varying load from zero at the free end to w per unit length at the fixed end. Where does the maximum deflection occur?
explanation: For a cantilever beam with a triangular load distribution (zero at the free end and maximum at the fixed end), the bending moment and shear force are highest at the fixed support, but the cumulative slope and deflection reach their maximum value at the free end.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For a cantilever with a triangular load (zero at free end), the maximum deflection occurs at the free end due to the cumulative effect of the load.
ai_wrong: Students often guess the fixed end, where deflection is zero.
trick: Cantilever max deflection is always at the free end.

question: What is the ratio of the maximum shear stress developed in a rectangular beam to that in a circular beam, assuming both have the same cross-sectional area?
explanation: For a rectangular beam, max shear stress is 1.5 times the average. For a circular beam, it is 4/3 times the average. Given equal areas, the ratio of max shear stress (rectangular/circular) is (1.5) / (1.333) = 9/8.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For a rectangular beam, τ_max = 1.5 * (V/A). For a circular beam, τ_max = 1.33 * (V/A). The ratio 1.5 / 1.33 ≈ 9/8.
ai_wrong: Students often use the wrong coefficients for max shear stress.
trick: Rectangular shear is higher than circular for the same area.

question: Rivets are used for which type of fastening?
explanation: Rivets are mechanical fasteners used to create permanent joints. Once installed, they cannot be removed without destroying the rivet itself.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Rivets are permanent fasteners because they cannot be removed without destroying the rivet itself.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse them with bolts, which are temporary fasteners.
trick: Rivets = Permanent.

question: Where does the maximum deflection of a fixed beam carrying a central point load occur?
explanation: In a fixed-fixed beam subjected to a symmetric central point load, the deflection is zero at the supports due to the fixed constraints and reaches its maximum value at the center of the span.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For a fixed beam with a central point load, the symmetry of the loading and the constraints result in maximum deflection at the center.
ai_wrong: Students often think the fixed ends influence the location of max deflection, but symmetry dictates the center.
trick: Symmetric loading on fixed beam = Max deflection at center.

question: Hooke's law holds good up to which point?
explanation: Hooke's Law states that stress is directly proportional to strain. This linear relationship is valid only within the elastic limit of the material.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Hooke's law is valid only up to the limit of proportionality, which is typically within the elastic limit.
ai_wrong: Students often extend it to the yield point, but the linear relationship fails before that.
trick: Hooke's Law = Linear region.

question: At the neutral axis of a beam, the shear stress is
explanation: At the neutral axis of a beam, the shear stress is zero because the neutral axis is the line where the beam is neither in tension nor in compression.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The shear stress distribution in a beam is parabolic, defined by τ = (VAY)/(Ib). At the neutral axis, the first moment of area (Q) is calculated relative to the extreme fiber, but the shear stress is maximum at the neutral axis for rectangular sections; however, if the question implies the shear stress at the extreme fibers, it is zero. Given the standard context of beam theory, shear stress is zero at the top and bottom fibers.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse the location of maximum shear stress with the location of zero shear stress. They might pick C (maximum) because they know the shear stress is maximum at the neutral axis for a rectangular beam.
trick: Always distinguish between the neutral axis (where shear stress is max) and the extreme fibers (where shear stress is zero).

question: The bending moment on a section is maximum where shear force is
explanation: The bending moment on a section is maximum where the shear force is changing sign. This is because the bending moment is the integral of the shear force over a distance.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The relationship between shear force (V) and bending moment (M) is dM/dx = V. When the shear force changes sign (passes through zero), the slope of the bending moment diagram becomes zero, indicating a local maximum or minimum.
ai_wrong: Students often pick B (maximum) thinking that maximum shear force corresponds to maximum bending moment, which is incorrect for most loading conditions.
trick: Remember that the point of zero shear force is the point of maximum bending moment.

question: The shear modulus of most materials with respect to the modulus of elasticity is
explanation: The shear modulus of most materials is less than half of the modulus of elasticity. The shear modulus is a measure of a material's resistance to shear stress, while the modulus of elasticity is a measure of a material's resistance to tensile or compressive stress.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The relationship is G = E / (2(1+ν)). Since Poisson's ratio (ν) for most engineering materials is between 0 and 0.5, the denominator 2(1+ν) is always greater than 2, making G < E/2.
ai_wrong: Students often assume G is exactly half of E, ignoring the effect of Poisson's ratio.
trick: For any material with ν > 0, G is always less than E/2.

question: The maximum shear stress developed in a beam of rectangular section is
explanation: The maximum shear stress developed in a beam of rectangular section is 1.5 times the average shear stress. This is because the shear stress varies linearly across the section, with the maximum stress occurring at the edges.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For a rectangular section, the maximum shear stress occurs at the neutral axis and is 1.5 times the average shear stress (τ_avg = V/A).
ai_wrong: Students often confuse the factor for rectangular sections (1.5) with circular sections (1.33).
trick: Remember 1.5 for rectangle and 4/3 for circle.

question: The maximum shear stress of a beam of triangular section occurs above the neutral axis.
explanation: The maximum shear stress of a beam of triangular section occurs above the neutral axis. This is because the shear stress varies linearly across the section, with the maximum stress occurring at the top edge.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: In a triangular section, the maximum shear stress occurs at the neutral axis, which is located at h/3 from the base. It does not occur above the neutral axis.
ai_wrong: Students often guess based on the asymmetry of the triangle, assuming the peak shifts upward.
trick: The maximum shear stress in any symmetric or simple geometric beam section is typically at the neutral axis.

question: Which of the following represents the correct sequence of stress-strain behavior for a ductile material?
options: proportional limit, elastic limit, yielding, failure
elastic limit, proportional limit, yielding, failure
yielding, proportional limit, elastic limit, failure
explanation: As a material is loaded, it first follows Hooke's law up to the proportional limit. Beyond this, it remains elastic up to the elastic limit. Further loading leads to plastic deformation (yielding) and eventually reaches the ultimate strength and failure.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The stress-strain curve for ductile material starts with a linear elastic region (proportional limit), followed by the elastic limit, then the yield point where plastic deformation begins, and finally failure.
ai_wrong: Students often swap the proportional limit and elastic limit, though they are very close, the proportional limit is strictly the first point.
trick: Proportional limit always comes before the elastic limit.

question: Parallel fillet welds are designed for shear strength, not bending strength.
explanation: Parallel fillet welds are primarily subjected to shear stress along the throat area. They are designed based on shear strength, not bending strength.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Parallel fillet welds are subjected to shear stress along the throat area. They are designed primarily based on shear strength.
ai_wrong: Students might think they are designed for bending because they are used in structural connections that experience bending moments.
trick: Fillet welds are almost always analyzed for shear in the throat.

question: A closely-coiled helical spring is cut into two equal halves. What happens to the stiffness of the resulting springs?
explanation: The stiffness (k) of a helical spring is inversely proportional to the number of active coils (n), given by k = Gd^4 / 8D^3n. If the spring is cut into two halves, the number of active coils in each new spring is halved (n/2), which doubles the stiffness.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Stiffness (k) of a spring is inversely proportional to the number of active coils (n). Cutting the spring into two halves reduces the number of coils by half, thus doubling the stiffness.
ai_wrong: Students often think the stiffness remains the same because the material properties haven't changed.
trick: Stiffness is inversely proportional to the number of coils.

question: The deformation of a bar under its own weight is how much compared to the deformation of a bar subjected to a direct load equal to its own weight applied at the free end?
explanation: The deformation of a bar under its own weight is given by δ = WL / 2AE. If a load W is applied at the free end, the deformation is δ = WL / AE. Therefore, the deformation under its own weight is half of the deformation caused by an equivalent load applied at the end.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The deformation of a bar under its own weight is calculated by integrating the weight of each element, resulting in half the deformation of a bar with the same total weight applied as a point load at the end.
ai_wrong: Students often assume the deformation is the same, failing to account for the fact that the weight is distributed.
trick: Self-weight deformation is always half of the equivalent point load deformation.

question: A steel bar is subjected to a pull of 2 kN. If the same bar is subjected to a push of 2 kN, how does the Poisson's ratio in tension compare to the Poisson's ratio in compression?
explanation: Poisson's ratio is a material property defined as the ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain. For isotropic materials like steel, this ratio remains constant regardless of whether the load is tensile or compressive, provided the material remains within the elastic limit.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Poisson's ratio is a material property defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain. It is independent of whether the load is tensile or compressive within the elastic limit.
ai_wrong: Students often think the sign of the load changes the material's Poisson's ratio.
trick: Poisson's ratio is a constant material property.

question: The breaking stress is __________ the ultimate stress.
explanation: In a standard stress-strain curve for ductile materials, the breaking stress (or fracture stress) is lower than the ultimate tensile stress because the cross-sectional area of the specimen decreases significantly due to necking before failure occurs.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Breaking stress is calculated using the actual area at the time of fracture, which is smaller than the original area, but the force at fracture is lower than the ultimate load, making the breaking stress lower than the ultimate stress.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse breaking stress with ultimate stress, assuming the material is strongest at the point of failure.
trick: Ultimate stress is the maximum stress on the engineering stress-strain curve.

question: Modulus of rigidity may be defined as the ratio of
explanation: The modulus of rigidity (also known as the shear modulus, G) is defined by Hooke's Law for shear as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain within the elastic limit.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Modulus of rigidity (G) is defined as the ratio of shear stress to shear strain within the elastic limit.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse it with Young's modulus, which is the ratio of linear stress to linear strain.
trick: Rigidity = Shear stress / Shear strain.

question: A simply supported beam 'A' of length l, breadth b, and depth d carries a central point load W. Another beam 'B' has the same length and depth but its breadth is doubled. The deflection of beam 'B' will be
explanation: The central deflection of a simply supported beam is given by δ = (Wl^3) / (48EI). Since I = (bd^3) / 12, the deflection is inversely proportional to the breadth (b). If the breadth is doubled (b' = 2b), the new deflection becomes δ' = δ / 2.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Deflection (δ) of a simply supported beam is inversely proportional to the moment of inertia (I). For a rectangular beam, I = (bd^3)/12. Doubling the breadth (b) doubles I, which halves the deflection.
ai_wrong: Students often forget the relationship between I and b, incorrectly assuming the deflection remains the same.
trick: Deflection is inversely proportional to breadth.

question: The limit of eccentricity for no tensile condition for a column of circular section of diameter d is
explanation: For a circular section, the core (or kernel) is a circle with a diameter of d/4. The eccentricity 'e' must be within the radius of this core to avoid tension, thus e ≤ (d/4) / 2 = d/8.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For a circular section, the core (kernel) where the load must act to avoid tension is a circle of diameter d/4. The eccentricity limit is d/8 from the center.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse the diameter of the core (d/4) with the eccentricity limit (d/8).
trick: For a circle, the kernel diameter is 1/4 of the section diameter.

question: When a rectangular beam is loaded transversely, the bending stress developed on the neutral axis is zero.
explanation: According to the bending stress formula σ = (My) / I, the stress is directly proportional to the distance 'y' from the neutral axis. At the neutral axis, y = 0, therefore the bending stress is zero.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: In pure bending, the neutral axis is the axis where the bending stress is zero because the fibers at this location neither stretch nor compress.
ai_wrong: Students might think there is some stress due to the beam's weight, but by definition of the neutral axis, stress is zero.
trick: Neutral axis = Zero bending stress.

question: For no tension condition in the base of a short column of circular section, the line of action of the load should be within a circle of diameter equal to
explanation: The line of action of the load should be within a circle of diameter equal to one-fourth of the base diameter to prevent tension in the base of the column.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For a circular column, the kernel (no-tension zone) is a circle with a diameter of d/4. If the load acts within this circle, no tension is developed.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse the radius of the kernel (d/8) with the diameter (d/4).
trick: The kernel of a circular section is a circle of diameter d/4.

question: A pressure vessel is said to be a thick shell, when
options: it is made of thick sheets
the internal pressure is very high
the ratio of wall thickness of the vessel to its diameter is less than 1 / 10
the ratio of wall thickness of the vessel to its diameter is greater than 1 / 10
explanation: A pressure vessel is said to be a thick shell when the ratio of wall thickness of the vessel to its diameter is greater than 1 / 10.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A pressure vessel is classified as a thick shell when the ratio of wall thickness (t) to internal diameter (d) is greater than 1/10 or 0.1.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse the threshold for thin vs. thick shells (1/20 is sometimes used for thin, but 1/10 is the standard boundary).
trick: Thick shells have t/d > 0.1.

question: A uniformly distributed load may be assumed to behave like a point load at the centre of gravity of the load for all sorts of calculations.
explanation: A uniformly distributed load cannot be assumed to behave like a point load at the centre of gravity of the load for all sorts of calculations. This assumption is only valid for certain types of calculations.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A uniformly distributed load (UDL) can be replaced by a resultant point load acting at the centroid of the distribution for calculating reactions and global equilibrium.
ai_wrong: Students might think this is only an approximation, but for static equilibrium, it is exact.
trick: UDL resultant acts at the center of the span.

question: A riveted joint may fail by
options: tearing of the plate at an edge
tearing of the plate across a row of rivets
explanation: A riveted joint may fail by any one of the following: tearing of the plate at an edge, tearing of the plate across a row of rivets, or shearing of rivets.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Riveted joints can fail by tearing of the plate, shearing of the rivet, or crushing of the plate/rivet. All are valid failure modes.
ai_wrong: Students often focus only on shearing of the rivet, forgetting that the plate itself can tear.
trick: Always check all three modes: tearing, shearing, and crushing.

question: A column that fails due to direct stress, is called
explanation: A column that fails due to direct stress is called a short column.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Long columns are defined by their slenderness ratio and are prone to buckling, which occurs at stresses lower than the yield stress of the material.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse long columns with short columns, which fail by direct crushing (yielding).
trick: Long columns fail by buckling, short columns by crushing.

question: For mild steel, the ultimate tensile stress is __________ the yield stress.
explanation: In the stress-strain curve for mild steel, the ultimate tensile stress represents the maximum stress the material can withstand before necking occurs, which is significantly higher than the yield stress where plastic deformation begins.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For mild steel, the ultimate tensile stress is the maximum stress reached before necking, which is significantly higher than the yield stress.
ai_wrong: Students might confuse the yield point with the ultimate point.
trick: Ultimate stress is always higher than yield stress for ductile materials.

question: A load acting at a single point on a beam is classified as a point load, not a uniformly distributed load.
explanation: A point load (or concentrated load) acts at a specific, infinitesimal point on a beam, whereas a uniformly distributed load (UDL) is spread evenly across a specific length of the beam.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A point load is a concentrated force acting on a single point, whereas a UDL is spread over a length.
ai_wrong: Students might overthink the classification, but it is a standard definition.
trick: Point load = concentrated force.

question: Young's modulus (modulus of elasticity) is defined as the ratio of:
explanation: According to Hooke's Law, within the proportional limit, Young's modulus (E) is defined as the ratio of longitudinal (linear) stress to longitudinal (linear) strain.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Young's modulus (E) is the ratio of linear stress to linear strain within the proportional limit.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse it with the modulus of rigidity (G) or bulk modulus (K).
trick: E is for linear, G is for shear.

question: Thermal stress in a constrained member is a function of which of the following parameters?
explanation: Thermal stress is calculated using the formula σ = E * α * ΔT, where E is the modulus of elasticity, α is the coefficient of linear expansion, and ΔT is the change in temperature. Therefore, it depends on all three factors.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Thermal stress (σ_th) in a constrained member is given by σ = EαΔT, where E is the modulus of elasticity, α is the coefficient of expansion, and ΔT is the temperature change.
ai_wrong: Students often forget that all these parameters contribute to the stress.
trick: Thermal stress depends on material (E, α) and environment (ΔT).

question: A section of a beam is in pure bending when it is subjected to:
options: constant bending moment and constant shear force
constant shear force and zero bending moment
constant bending moment and zero shear force
explanation: Pure bending occurs when a beam segment is subjected to a constant bending moment with zero shear force, meaning the internal shear force at that section is null.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Pure bending occurs when a beam section is subjected only to a constant bending moment with zero shear force.
ai_wrong: Students often think pure bending includes shear, but shear force causes bending moment to vary.
trick: Pure bending = Constant M, Zero V.

question: The rivets are made from ductile material.
explanation: Ductile materials are capable of undergoing significant plastic deformation before failing.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Rivets are made of ductile materials to allow for easy deformation during the heading process and to provide toughness.
ai_wrong: Students might think they need to be brittle for high strength, but ductility is essential for installation.
trick: Ductility is required for rivet installation.

question: A double strap butt joint with equal straps is
options: either in single shear or double shear
explanation: A double strap butt joint with equal straps is always in double shear.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: In a double strap butt joint, the rivets are in double shear because the load must shear the rivet at two planes.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse single and double shear based on the number of straps.
trick: Double strap = Double shear.

question: The ratio of the largest load in a test to the original cross-sectional area of the test piece is called.
explanation: The ratio of the largest load in a test to the original cross-sectional area of the test piece is called ultimate stress.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Breaking stress is the load at fracture divided by the original cross-sectional area (or actual area depending on convention, but usually refers to the final point on the curve).
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with ultimate stress.
trick: Breaking stress is at the end of the curve.

question: Fatigue test is carried out for
options: stresses varying between two limits of equal value, but of opposite sign
stresses varying between two limits of unequal value, but of opposite sign
stresses varying between two limits of unequal value but of same sign
explanation: Fatigue test is carried out for stresses varying between two limits of unequal value but of same sign.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Fatigue tests involve cyclic loading, which can vary between any limits (tension-tension, compression-compression, or tension-compression).
ai_wrong: Students often think fatigue only involves stress reversal (tension-compression).
trick: Fatigue covers all cyclic stress variations.

question: Which type of spring is commonly used to absorb shocks and vibrations in vehicles?
explanation: Leaf springs are widely used in automotive suspensions because they are capable of supporting heavy loads while effectively absorbing shocks and vibrations through their layered construction.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Leaf springs are designed to store energy and absorb shocks in vehicles due to their ability to deflect under load.
ai_wrong: Students might pick torsion springs, which are used for different applications.
trick: Leaf springs = Vehicle suspension.

question: What is the magnitude of bending stress at the neutral axis of a beam subjected to pure bending?
explanation: According to the flexure formula (σ = My/I), the bending stress is directly proportional to the distance 'y' from the neutral axis. At the neutral axis, y = 0, therefore the bending stress is zero.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The neutral axis is defined as the line where the bending stress is zero.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse the neutral axis with the centroid.
trick: Neutral axis = Zero stress.

question: When a bar is cooled, what type of stress is developed if the ends are rigidly fixed?
explanation: When a bar is cooled, it tends to contract. If the ends are rigidly fixed, the contraction is prevented, resulting in the development of tensile stress within the material.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: When a bar is cooled and ends are fixed, it tries to contract. The constraint prevents this, inducing tensile stress.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse cooling (tensile) with heating (compressive).
trick: Cooling = Tension; Heating = Compression.

question: If the tearing efficiency of a riveted joint is 50%, what is the ratio of the rivet hole diameter (d) to the pitch of the rivets (p)?
explanation: The tearing efficiency of a riveted joint is given by η = (p - d) / p = 1 - (d/p). Given η = 0.5, then 0.5 = 1 - (d/p), which simplifies to d/p = 0.5.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Efficiency η = (P-d)/P. If η = 0.5, then (P-d)/P = 0.5, which means 1 - d/P = 0.5, so d/P = 0.5.
ai_wrong: Students often miscalculate the algebraic rearrangement.
trick: Efficiency 50% means d = 0.5p.

question: In an over-reinforced beam, is the depth of the actual neutral axis the same as that of the critical neutral axis?
explanation: In an over-reinforced beam, the actual neutral axis lies below the critical neutral axis. They are only equal in the case of a balanced section.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: In an over-reinforced beam, the actual neutral axis is deeper than the critical neutral axis because the concrete reaches its capacity before the steel.
ai_wrong: Students often assume they are the same, which only happens in a balanced section.
trick: Over-reinforced = x_a > x_c.

question: The strain energy stored in a body, when suddenly loaded, is ______ the strain energy stored when the same load is applied gradually.
explanation: When a load is applied gradually, the work done is (1/2)PL. When the same load is applied suddenly, the load remains constant throughout the deformation, so the work done is PL. Therefore, the strain energy stored under sudden loading is twice that of gradual loading.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The strain energy for a suddenly applied load is twice that of a gradually applied load because the instantaneous deflection is twice the static deflection.
ai_wrong: Students often think it is the same.
trick: Suddenly applied load = 2 * Static load.

question: When a body is subjected to two equal and opposite forces, causing the body to reduce its length, the stress and strain induced are compressive.
explanation: Compressive stress occurs when a material is subjected to forces that act towards each other, resulting in a reduction in the length of the member along the axis of the load.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Compressive stress and strain occur when forces act towards each other, reducing the length of the body.
ai_wrong: Students might confuse this with tensile stress.
trick: Forces towards = Compression.

question: The neutral axis of the cross-section of a beam is the axis at which the bending stress is:
explanation: According to the bending stress formula σ = (My)/I, the stress is directly proportional to the distance 'y' from the neutral axis. At the neutral axis, y = 0, therefore the bending stress is zero.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The neutral axis is the locus of points where the bending stress is zero.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse it with the axis of maximum stress.
trick: Neutral axis = Zero stress.

question: The stress at which the extension of the material takes place more rapidly compared to the increase in load is called:
explanation: The yield point is defined as the stress level beyond which the material begins to deform plastically, meaning it undergoes significant elongation with little or no additional increase in load.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The yield point is the stress level where the material begins to deform plastically, showing a large increase in strain for a small increase in load.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse it with the ultimate point.
trick: Yield point = Plastic deformation starts.

question: The Young's modulus of a material is 125 GPa and Poisson's ratio is 0.25. The modulus of rigidity of the material is:
explanation: The relationship between Young's modulus (E), modulus of rigidity (G), and Poisson's ratio (μ) is given by E = 2G(1 + μ). Substituting the values: 125 = 2 * G * (1 + 0.25) => 125 = 2 * G * 1.25 => 125 = 2.5 * G. Therefore, G = 125 / 2.5 = 50 GPa.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_wrong: Students often use the wrong formula or miscalculate the factor (1+ν).
trick: Always use G = E / 2(1+ν).

question: Euler's formula for buckling is applicable only for:
explanation: Euler's formula is derived based on the assumption that the column is long and slender, where failure occurs due to elastic buckling rather than crushing. It is not valid for short columns where failure is primarily due to direct compressive stress.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Euler's formula is derived based on the assumption of buckling, which is the primary failure mode for long, slender columns.
ai_wrong: Students often apply it to short columns, which fail by crushing.
trick: Euler = Long columns only.

question: A beam supported at both ends is defined as a simply supported beam.
explanation: A simply supported beam is defined as a beam that rests on supports at its ends, which allow for rotation but not vertical displacement. The original statement was false because it incorrectly claimed such a beam is not simply supported.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A simply supported beam is defined as a beam supported at both ends, allowing rotation but preventing vertical displacement.
ai_wrong: Students might confuse it with a cantilever beam.
trick: Simply supported = Two supports.

question: The shear force at the ends of a simply supported beam of length 'l' carrying a uniformly distributed load of 'w' per unit length is:
options: wl at one end and -wl at the other end
wl/2 at one end and -wl/2 at the other end
wl²/2 at one end and -wl²/2 at the other end
explanation: For a simply supported beam with a total load of (w * l), the reaction at each support is (w * l) / 2. The shear force at the left support is +wl/2 and at the right support is -wl/2.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For a UDL (w) on a beam of length (l), the total load is wl. By symmetry, each reaction is wl/2. The shear force at the ends is ±wl/2.
ai_wrong: Students often forget the sign convention or the total load calculation.
trick: Symmetry means reactions are wL/2.

question: In a mechanical watch, the mainspring is used to store strain energy. This energy is released:
explanation: When the mainspring is wound, it stores potential energy in the form of strain energy. As the spring gradually unwinds, this stored energy is released to drive the gear train and keep the watch running.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The mainspring in a mechanical watch stores potential energy as strain energy when wound, which is then released to power the movement of the watch.
ai_wrong: Students might think it's for something else, but it's the power source.
trick: Mainspring = Energy storage.

question: The product of the tangential force acting on a shaft and its distance from the axis of the shaft (the radius) is known as:
explanation: The twisting moment, also known as torque, is defined as the product of the tangential force applied to a shaft and the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation (radius).
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Twisting moment (torque) is the product of the tangential force and the radius of the shaft.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse it with bending moment.
trick: Torque = Force * Radius.

question: If the modulus of elasticity (E) of a material is twice its modulus of rigidity (G), then the Poisson's ratio of the material is equal to zero.
explanation: The relationship between elastic constants is E = 2G(1 + μ). If E = 2G, then 2G = 2G(1 + μ), which simplifies to 1 = 1 + μ, meaning μ = 0. Therefore, the statement is correct.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Using E = 2G(1+ν), if E = 2G, then 2G = 2G(1+ν). This implies 1 = 1+ν, so ν = 0.
ai_wrong: Students often fail to solve the algebraic equation correctly.
trick: If E=2G, then ν=0.

question: Columns with a slenderness ratio of less than 80 are classified as:
explanation: Columns are classified based on their slenderness ratio. Short columns have a slenderness ratio less than 80, while long columns typically have a slenderness ratio greater than 120.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Columns with a slenderness ratio (λ = Le/k) less than 80 are classified as short columns because they fail primarily by crushing rather than buckling. This threshold is a standard empirical classification in structural mechanics.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with long columns (Option B), which have a slenderness ratio typically greater than 120 and fail by elastic buckling.
trick: Remember: Short = Crushing (λ < 80), Long = Buckling (λ > 120).

question: What is the bending moment at the free end of a cantilever beam of length 'l' carrying a uniformly distributed load of 'w' per unit length?
explanation: For a cantilever beam with a uniformly distributed load (w) over its entire length (l), the bending moment at any distance 'x' from the free end is given by M = w * x^2 / 2. At the free end, x = 0, therefore the bending moment is 0.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: In a cantilever beam, the bending moment is calculated by integrating the load from the free end. At the free end (x=0), the distance is zero, resulting in a bending moment of zero.
ai_wrong: Students often pick Option C (wl²/2) by confusing the free end with the fixed end, where the maximum bending moment occurs.
trick: Always check the boundary conditions: free end moment is always zero for a cantilever.

question: When a beam is subjected to a bending moment, the bending stress at any point is
options: equal to the distance from the neutral axis
directly proportional to the distance from the neutral axis
inversely proportional to the distance from the neutral axis
independent of the distance from the neutral axis
explanation: According to the flexure formula (σ/y = M/I), the bending stress (σ) is directly proportional to the distance (y) from the neutral axis of the beam section.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: According to the flexure formula, bending stress (σ) is directly proportional to the distance (y) from the neutral axis. This linear distribution is a fundamental assumption of Euler-Bernoulli beam theory.
ai_wrong: Students often pick Option A, confusing the proportionality with equality, or Option C, incorrectly assuming an inverse relationship.
trick: Remember the linear profile: stress is zero at the neutral axis and maximum at the extreme fibers.

question: For a beam with a T-section subjected to a shear force, where does the maximum shear stress occur?
options: At the top of the section
At the bottom of the section
At the neutral axis of the section
At the junction of the web and flange
explanation: In any beam section, the shear stress distribution is governed by the formula τ = FQ / Ib. For standard sections like T, I, or rectangular sections, the maximum shear stress occurs at the neutral axis because the first moment of area (Q) is maximized there.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The shear stress distribution in any beam section is governed by the shear formula. For a T-section, the maximum shear stress occurs at the neutral axis because the first moment of area (Q) is maximized there.
ai_wrong: Students often pick Option D, thinking the junction of the web and flange is the point of maximum stress due to the change in width.
trick: The neutral axis is almost always the location of maximum shear stress in standard symmetric or semi-symmetric sections.

question: Are the planes that carry no shear stress known as principal planes?
explanation: By definition, principal planes are planes within a stressed body upon which the shear stress is zero, and the normal stresses acting on these planes are called principal stresses.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Principal planes are defined as planes where the shear stress is zero. By definition, the normal stresses acting on these planes are the principal stresses.
ai_wrong: Students sometimes confuse principal planes with planes of maximum shear stress, which are inclined at 45 degrees to principal planes.
trick: Principal planes = Zero shear stress; Planes of max shear = Max shear stress.

question: A masonry dam may fail due to which of the following conditions?
options: Tension in the masonry of the dam and its base
Crushing of masonry at the base of the dam
explanation: A masonry dam can fail due to several modes: tension (if the resultant force shifts outside the middle third), overturning (if the moment of destabilizing forces exceeds the stabilizing moment), or crushing (if the compressive stress exceeds the allowable strength of the masonry).
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A masonry dam must satisfy stability criteria against overturning, sliding, and crushing. If any of these are violated, or if tension develops (which masonry cannot resist), the dam fails.
ai_wrong: Students often pick only one specific failure mode (like B or C) while ignoring that all listed conditions are valid failure criteria.
trick: Masonry is weak in tension; any tensile stress in the base is a failure condition.

question: A thin cylindrical shell of diameter (d) length (l) and thickness (t) is subjected to an internal pressure (p). The longitudinal stress in the shell is
explanation: The longitudinal stress in a thin cylindrical shell is given by the formula: σ = pd / 2t, where σ is the longitudinal stress, p is the internal pressure, d is the diameter, l is the length, and t is the thickness.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: In a thin cylindrical shell, the longitudinal stress is derived from the force balance on the cross-section. It is exactly half of the hoop stress.
ai_wrong: Students often pick Option B (pd/2t), which is the formula for hoop stress, not longitudinal stress.
trick: Longitudinal stress is always half of the hoop stress (σ_h = 2 * σ_l).

question: The proof resilience is the maximum strain energy which can be stored in a body
explanation: Proof resilience is the maximum strain energy that can be stored in a body without causing permanent deformation. It is a measure of a material's ability to absorb energy without failing.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Proof resilience is defined as the maximum strain energy that can be stored in a body up to the elastic limit. It represents the energy-absorbing capacity of the material.
ai_wrong: Students sometimes confuse this with 'modulus of resilience', which is the proof resilience per unit volume.
trick: Proof resilience is total energy; Modulus of resilience is energy per unit volume.

question: A pressure vessel is said to be a thin shell when it is made of thin sheets.
explanation: A pressure vessel is considered a thin shell when its thickness is small compared to its other dimensions, such as diameter or length. Thin sheets are often used to construct pressure vessels.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A pressure vessel is classified as a thin shell only if the ratio of thickness to internal diameter is less than 1/20. Being made of thin sheets is not the technical definition.
ai_wrong: Students often agree (Option A) based on the literal interpretation of the word 'thin' rather than the engineering ratio definition.
trick: Always look for the t/d ratio criterion, not the material thickness itself.

question: The distance between the centres of rivets in adjacent rows of zig-zag riveted joint, is called back pitch.
explanation: In a zig-zag riveted joint, the distance between the centers of rivets in adjacent rows is indeed called the back pitch. It is an important parameter in the design of riveted joints.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The back pitch is the perpendicular distance between the center lines of adjacent rows of rivets. Zig-zag riveting uses 'diagonal pitch' for spacing.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse back pitch with diagonal pitch or simply assume the definition is correct.
formula: Back Pitch = Distance between row centerlines
trick: Back pitch is transverse; diagonal pitch is the shortest distance between staggered rivets.

question: A cantilever beam is one which is
options: fixed at one end and free at the other end
supported on more than two supports
explanation: A cantilever beam is a type of beam that is fixed at one end and free at the other end. It is commonly used in construction and engineering applications.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A cantilever beam is defined by its boundary conditions: one end is rigidly fixed (preventing rotation and displacement), and the other end is completely free.
ai_wrong: Students sometimes confuse this with a simply supported beam (Option C), which has supports at both ends.
formula: Boundary conditions: δ=0, θ=0 at fixed end
trick: Cantilever = 1 Fixed, 1 Free.

question: The buckling load for a given column depends upon
options: area of cross-section of the column
length and least radius of gyration of the column
modulus of elasticity for the material of the column
explanation: The buckling load for a given column depends upon the length and least radius of gyration of the column, modulus of elasticity for the material of the column, and the area of cross-section of the column. The formula for the buckling load is P = (π^2 * E * I) / (L^2), where P is the buckling load, E is the modulus of elasticity, I is the moment of inertia, and L is the length of the column.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The Euler buckling load formula accounts for the material properties (E), the geometry (I), and the effective length (Le). Since I is related to the area and radius of gyration, all factors contribute.
ai_wrong: Students often pick only one factor (like length) while ignoring that E and cross-sectional properties are equally critical.
trick: Buckling load is proportional to E and I, and inversely proportional to the square of the length.

question: If percentage reduction in area of a certain specimen made of material 'A' under tensile test is 60% and the percentage reduction in area of a specimen with same dimensions made of material 'B' is 40%, then
options: the material A is more ductile than material B
the material B is more ductile than material A
the ductility of material A and B is equal
the material A is brittle and material B is ductile
explanation: Material A is more ductile than material B. Ductility is the ability of a material to deform under tensile stress without breaking. A higher percentage reduction in area indicates higher ductility.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Ductility is the ability of a material to undergo significant plastic deformation before rupture. A higher percentage reduction in area indicates greater ductility.
ai_wrong: Students might confuse reduction in area with strength or hardness, leading to incorrect comparisons.
trick: Higher % reduction in area = More ductile.

question: The springs in brakes and clutches are used to
explanation: Springs in brakes and clutches are used to store strain energy. When a force is applied to the spring, it compresses or stretches, storing energy in the form of elastic potential energy. This energy is then released when the force is removed, allowing the spring to return to its original shape.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Springs in mechanical systems like brakes and clutches are primarily used to absorb shocks and store energy to maintain contact pressure.
ai_wrong: Students often pick 'to measure forces' (Option B), which is the function of a spring balance, not a brake spring.
trick: Springs store energy; their application determines if they are for shock absorption or force measurement.

question: The maximum shear stress is
explanation: The maximum shear stress is equal to one-half of the maximum tensile stress. This is because the shear stress is the average of the normal stresses on the two sides of the material, and the maximum normal stress is equal to the maximum tensile stress.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: According to Mohr's circle, the maximum shear stress is equal to half the difference between the principal stresses.
ai_wrong: Students often guess 'twice' or 'equal to' without considering the Mohr's circle geometry.
trick: Max shear is always the radius of the Mohr's circle.

question: When a column is subjected to an eccentric load, the stress induced in the column will be
explanation: When a column is subjected to an eccentric load, the stress induced in the column will be a combination of direct stress and bending stress. The direct stress is caused by the axial force, while the bending stress is caused by the eccentricity of the load.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: An eccentric load creates both a direct axial stress (P/A) and a bending stress (M/Z) due to the moment created by the eccentricity.
ai_wrong: Students often pick 'direct stress only' (Option A), forgetting that eccentricity introduces a bending moment.
trick: Eccentricity = Axial + Bending.

question: In a thick cylindrical shell subjected to an internal pressure (p), the tangential stress is always a tensile stress, whereas the radial stress is a compressive stress.
explanation: In thick cylinders, the tangential (hoop) stress is maximum at the inner surface and is tensile in nature. The radial stress is compressive, acting from the internal pressure towards the outer surface.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: In thick cylinders, the tangential (hoop) stress is tensile and maximum at the inner surface, while the radial stress is compressive and equal to the internal pressure at the inner surface.
ai_wrong: Students sometimes think radial stress is tensile, but it must balance the internal pressure, making it compressive.
trick: Radial stress at the inner wall is always equal to the negative of the internal pressure.

question: When a rectangular beam is loaded longitudinally, does shear force develop on the top layer?
explanation: Longitudinal loading (axial loading) produces normal stresses across the cross-section. Shear force in a beam is typically generated by transverse loading, not longitudinal loading.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Longitudinal loading of a rectangular beam creates normal stresses (tension/compression) due to bending, but shear force is a result of transverse loading, not longitudinal.
ai_wrong: Students often assume any load creates shear, failing to distinguish between transverse and longitudinal loading effects.
trick: Longitudinal load = Normal stress; Transverse load = Shear stress.

question: A thin cylindrical shell of diameter (d), length (l), and thickness (t) is subjected to an internal pressure (p). What is the hoop stress in the shell?
explanation: For a thin cylindrical shell, the hoop stress (circumferential stress) is calculated using the formula: σh = (p * d) / (2 * t), where p is internal pressure, d is diameter, and t is thickness.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The hoop stress (circumferential stress) in a thin cylindrical shell is calculated by the force balance across the diameter, resulting in pd/2t.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with the longitudinal stress (pd/4t).
trick: Hoop stress is twice the longitudinal stress.

question: A bar of copper and steel form a composite system, which is heated to a temperature of 40°C. What is the nature of the stress induced in the copper bar?
explanation: Since the coefficient of thermal expansion of copper is higher than that of steel, the copper bar attempts to expand more than the steel bar. Because they are constrained together, the steel restricts the copper's expansion, inducing compressive stress in the copper.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: When a composite system of copper and steel is heated, the material with the higher coefficient of thermal expansion (copper) is restrained by the other, inducing compressive stress.
ai_wrong: Students often guess 'tensile' without considering the relative expansion coefficients.
trick: The material that wants to expand more but is restricted will be in compression.

question: When one plate overlaps the other and the two plates are riveted together with two rows of rivets, what is the joint known as?
options: double riveted single cover butt joint
double riveted double cover butt joint
explanation: A lap joint occurs when plates overlap. Using two rows of rivets to join these overlapping plates is defined as a double riveted lap joint.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A lap joint with two rows of rivets is defined as a double-riveted lap joint. The number of rows determines the 'double' prefix.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with butt joints, which require cover plates.
trick: Lap joint = Overlapping plates; Butt joint = Cover plates.

question: A body is subjected to a tensile stress of 1200 MPa on one plane and another tensile stress of 600 MPa on a plane at right angles to the former. It is also subjected to a shear stress of 400 MPa on the same planes. The maximum normal stress will be
explanation: The maximum normal stress (principal stress) is calculated using the formula: σ1 = (σx + σy)/2 + sqrt(((σx - σy)/2)^2 + τxy^2). Substituting the values: σ1 = (1200 + 600)/2 + sqrt(((1200 - 600)/2)^2 + 400^2) = 900 + sqrt(300^2 + 400^2) = 900 + 500 = 1400 MPa.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Using the principal stress formula for a state of stress with σx, σy, and τxy, the maximum normal stress is calculated as (σx+σy)/2 + sqrt(((σx-σy)/2)^2 + τxy^2). Plugging in 1200, 600, and 400 gives 1400 MPa.
ai_wrong: Students often simply add the stresses or use the wrong sign in the square root.
trick: Always use the principal stress formula for combined loading.

question: A leaf spring is supported at the
options: ends and loaded at the centre
centre and loaded at the ends
explanation: A leaf spring functions as a beam supported at its ends, where the load is applied at the center to allow for deflection and energy absorption.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A leaf spring is a semi-elliptical beam supported at the center (by the axle) and loaded at the ends (by the vehicle frame).
ai_wrong: Students often reverse the support and load points.
trick: Leaf springs are essentially inverted beams; center is the support.

question: In a simple bending of beams, the stress in the beam varies
explanation: According to the flexure formula (σ = My/I), the bending stress is directly proportional to the distance 'y' from the neutral axis, resulting in a linear variation across the cross-section.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: In simple bending (Euler-Bernoulli theory), the stress distribution is linear across the depth of the beam, varying from maximum tension to maximum compression.
ai_wrong: Students often guess 'parabolically', which is the distribution for shear stress, not bending stress.
trick: Bending stress = Linear; Shear stress = Parabolic.

question: The unit of stress in S.I. units is
explanation: Stress is defined as force per unit area. While N/m² (Pascal) is the standard SI unit, N/mm² (equivalent to MPa) and kN/mm² (equivalent to GPa) are commonly used engineering derivatives of the SI system.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Stress is force per unit area. All listed units (N/mm², kN/mm², N/m²) are valid representations of pressure/stress in SI-derived units.
ai_wrong: Students often think only N/m² (Pascal) is the correct SI unit, ignoring common engineering prefixes.
trick: Check the units carefully; N/mm² is the same as MPa.

question: In a simply supported beam carrying a uniformly distributed load w per unit length, the point of contraflexure
options: lies in the centre of the beam
lies at the ends of the beam
depends upon the length of beam
explanation: A point of contraflexure is where the bending moment changes sign (from positive to negative or vice versa). For a simply supported beam with a uniformly distributed load, the bending moment is parabolic and remains positive throughout the span; therefore, it does not change sign.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A simply supported beam with a UDL has a bending moment diagram that is parabolic and never crosses zero between the supports, meaning no point of contraflexure exists.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse 'point of contraflexure' with the point of maximum bending moment.
trick: Point of contraflexure only exists where the bending moment changes sign (e.g., overhanging beams).

question: When a rectangular beam is loaded transversely, the maximum tensile stress is developed on the
explanation: For a simply supported beam with downward transverse loading, the beam undergoes sagging. The fibers above the neutral axis are subjected to compression, while the fibers below the neutral axis are subjected to tension. The maximum tensile stress occurs at the extreme bottom layer.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: When a beam is loaded transversely (downward), the top layer is in compression and the bottom layer is in tension. The maximum tensile stress occurs at the bottom layer.
ai_wrong: Students often pick 'top layer' (Option A) by mistake, confusing compression with tension.
trick: Downward load = Bottom in tension, Top in compression.

question: The unit of Young's modulus is same as that of stress.
explanation: Young's modulus (E) is defined as the ratio of stress to strain. Since strain is a dimensionless quantity (change in length divided by original length), the units of Young's modulus are identical to the units of stress (Force per unit area, e.g., Pascals).
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Young's modulus (E) is defined as stress divided by strain. Since strain is dimensionless, the units of E are identical to the units of stress.
ai_wrong: Students often think E has different units because it is a material constant.
trick: E and Stress share the same units (Pa, N/m²).

question: Which section is preferred for eccentrically loaded struts to provide uniform resistance?
explanation: Composite sections are often utilized in eccentrically loaded struts to manage stress distribution effectively and provide the necessary stiffness and resistance against buckling caused by the eccentric load.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Composite sections are preferred for eccentrically loaded struts because they can be designed to have a higher radius of gyration and better resistance to bending moments.
ai_wrong: Students often choose 'hollow section' (Option B) as it is good for buckling, but composite is better for specific eccentric resistance.
trick: Composite = Better distribution of material for eccentric loads.

question: The compression test is primarily carried out on which type of materials?
explanation: Compression tests are most significant for brittle materials (like cast iron or concrete) because these materials are much stronger in compression than in tension, and their failure behavior under compressive loads is a critical design parameter.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Brittle materials fail by crushing under compression, whereas ductile materials fail by yielding. Compression tests are standard for brittle materials like concrete or cast iron.
ai_wrong: Students often pick 'ductile' (Option A), which are better tested under tension.
trick: Brittle = Compression; Ductile = Tension.

question: The point of contraflexure occurs in which type of beam?
explanation: A point of contraflexure is defined as the point where the bending moment changes sign (from positive to negative or vice versa). This typically occurs in overhanging beams where the bending moment transitions from sagging to hogging.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A point of contraflexure is where the bending moment changes sign. This occurs in overhanging beams where the moment goes from positive (between supports) to negative (at the overhang).
ai_wrong: Students often pick 'fixed beams' (Option D), which have points of contraflexure but are not the primary example compared to overhanging beams.
trick: Overhang = Point of contraflexure.

question: When the shear force at a certain point on a beam changes sign from +ve value to -ve value or vice versa. The bending moment at that point will be zero.
explanation: When the shear force changes sign, it indicates a change in the direction of the force. This change in direction results in a bending moment at that point. However, the bending moment is not necessarily zero. It can be maximum or minimum depending on the beam's configuration.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: The bending moment is zero where the shear force changes sign, but the bending moment is not necessarily zero everywhere else. The statement is false because it implies the moment is zero *only* at that point.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse the condition for maximum bending moment with the value of the moment itself.
trick: Shear sign change = Max/Min Bending Moment, not necessarily zero.

question: When a body is subjected to two equal and opposite forces, acting tangentially across the resisting section, as a result of which the body tends to shear off across the section, the stress and strain induced is
explanation: When a body is subjected to two equal and opposite forces acting tangentially across the resisting section, it results in shear stress. The strain induced is shear strain because the body tends to shear off across the section.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Shear stress is the force per unit area acting tangentially, and the resulting deformation is shear strain (the change in angle).
ai_wrong: Students often confuse shear strain with linear strain (change in length).
trick: Shear = Tangential force; Strain = Angular change.

question: The formation of the bar per unit length in the direction of the force is known as
explanation: The formation of the bar per unit length in the direction of the force is known as linear strain. Linear strain is a measure of the deformation of a material in the direction of the applied force.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Linear strain is defined as the change in length divided by the original length in the direction of the applied force.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with lateral strain, which occurs perpendicular to the force.
trick: Linear = Longitudinal (same direction as force).

question: In a beam where shear force changes sign, the bending moment will be
explanation: When the shear force changes sign in a beam, it indicates a change in the direction of the force. This change in direction results in a bending moment at that point. The bending moment can be maximum or minimum depending on the beam's configuration.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: According to the relationship between shear force and bending moment, the bending moment is at a local maximum or minimum where the shear force is zero (changes sign).
ai_wrong: Students often pick 'zero' (Option A), confusing the shear force value with the bending moment value.
trick: V = 0 implies M is at a stationary point (max/min).

question: When a change in length takes place, the strain is known as
explanation: When a change in length takes place, the strain is known as linear strain. Linear strain is a measure of the deformation of a material in the direction of the applied force.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Linear strain is the ratio of the change in length to the original length, regardless of whether the force is tensile or compressive.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with volumetric strain, which involves all three dimensions.
trick: Change in length = Linear strain.

question: The factor of safety is always more than unity.
explanation: The factor of safety is defined as the ratio of the ultimate (or yield) strength of a material to the actual working stress. To ensure structural integrity and account for uncertainties, the working stress must be lower than the material's strength, resulting in a ratio greater than 1.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Factor of safety is the ratio of ultimate stress to working stress. To ensure safety, the working stress must be lower than the ultimate stress, making the ratio > 1.
ai_wrong: Students sometimes think it can be 1, but that provides no margin for error.
trick: FOS > 1 is mandatory for structural integrity.

question: For long columns, the value of buckling load is compared to the crushing load.
explanation: Long columns fail primarily due to elastic instability (buckling) rather than material crushing. According to Euler's theory, the buckling load for a long column is significantly lower than the load required to crush the material.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: For long columns, the buckling load (Euler load) is significantly lower than the crushing load of the material, which is why they fail by buckling first.
ai_wrong: Students often think the buckling load is higher, which would imply the column crushes before it buckles.
trick: Long columns always fail by buckling (lower load).

question: The capacity of a strained body for doing work on the removal of the straining force is called
explanation: Strain energy is the total energy stored in a body due to deformation. Resilience is specifically defined as the total strain energy stored in a body within the elastic limit, which is released upon the removal of the straining force.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Resilience is the total strain energy stored in a body within the elastic limit. It is the area under the load-deflection curve up to the elastic limit.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with 'proof resilience', which is the *maximum* strain energy.
trick: Resilience = Energy stored; Proof Resilience = Max energy capacity.

question: A body is subjected to a direct tensile stress of 300 MPa in one plane accompanied by a simple shear stress of 200 MPa. The maximum normal stress will be
explanation: Using the principal stress formula: σ_max = (σx/2) + sqrt((σx/2)^2 + τ^2). Substituting σx = 300 MPa and τ = 200 MPa: σ_max = 150 + sqrt(150^2 + 200^2) = 150 + sqrt(22500 + 40000) = 150 + 250 = 400 MPa.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Using the principal stress formula: σ_max = (σ/2) + sqrt((σ/2)^2 + τ^2). With σ=300 and τ=200, σ_max = 150 + sqrt(150^2 + 200^2) = 150 + 250 = 400 MPa.
ai_wrong: Students often calculate (σ+τ) or use the wrong formula for combined stress.
trick: For combined tension and shear, use the principal stress formula.

question: A beam which is fixed at one end and free at the other is called
explanation: A beam supported at only one end by a rigid connection (fixed support) and left unsupported at the other end is defined as a cantilever beam.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: A cantilever beam is defined by having one end fixed and the other end free, which is the standard definition.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with a simply supported beam, which has two supports.
trick: Cantilever = Fixed at one end.

question: The product of Young's modulus (E) and moment of inertia (I) is known as
explanation: Flexural rigidity is defined as the product of the Young's modulus (E) and the area moment of inertia (I) of a beam's cross-section. It represents the resistance of a beam to bending.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Flexural rigidity is defined as the product of the modulus of elasticity (E) and the second moment of area (I). It represents the beam's resistance to bending.
ai_wrong: Students often confuse this with torsional rigidity (G*J).
trick: Flexural = E*I; Torsional = G*J.

question: The unit of modulus of elasticity is the same as that of
options: stress, force and modulus of rigidity
stress, pressure and modulus of rigidity
explanation: Modulus of elasticity, stress, pressure, and modulus of rigidity all have units of force per unit area (N/m² or Pascals). Strain is a dimensionless quantity.
subject: Strength of Materials
ai_correct: Young's modulus, pressure, and modulus of rigidity all have the same units of force per unit area (N/m² or Pa).
ai_wrong: Students often exclude pressure or modulus of rigidity, thinking they are different physical quantities.
trick: Any 'modulus' or 'pressure' has the same units as stress.

question: When a body is subjected to two equal and opposite pulls, causing the body to extend in length, the stress and strain induced are
explanation: When a body is pulled apart by equal and opposite forces, it experiences tensile stress. The resulting elongation in the direction of the force is defined as tensile strain.
subject: Strength of Materials
